summary: Win25H2Clean reversible proof still anchors the current-build story for StartupDelayInMSec: the observed baseline is missing, the app profile writes 0, the record retains Procmon shell-restart corroboration for Explorer\Serialize, a bounded WPR lane exercised both `missing` and `0`, and the value restores back to missing cleanly.

setting.name: Startup Program Delay
setting.casual_explanation: Windows can stagger startup apps instead of launching them all immediately. People disable the delay when they want login to feel quicker, but that can also make startup load spike harder.
setting.why_people_change_it: People disable it when they want startup apps to launch sooner after sign-in.
setting.tradeoffs_overview: Removing the startup delay can make apps appear sooner, but it can also increase boot-time contention and make sign-in feel heavier on slower systems. The current build evidence is strong, but this still is not a primary Microsoft registry publication.
setting.professional_notes: The current dataset now has four layers of proof for StartupDelayInMSec on HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\Serialize: repo notes, a Ghidra string-search hit for Explorer Serialize, a Win25H2Clean Procmon shell-restart capture reference for StartupDelayInMSec, and a bounded WPR lane that exercised both the missing baseline and the app's value `0` during Explorer restarts. The checked-in Procmon artifact is the repo-friendly `.pml` placeholder rather than a text export, and the Ghidra artifact is string-reference support rather than a decompiled registry-read function. The reversible VM probe still confirms missing -> 0 -> missing on the same path. Both WPR states kept `explorer`, `sihost`, `ShellHost`, and `ctfmon` alive after the shell restart. Under the current v3.1 contract that is still enough to keep the record on the converged-vm lane even though Microsoft does not publish the raw shell registry value as a primary supported policy surface.

allowed_values.label: Observed Win25H2Clean baseline
allowed_values.meaning: The current 25H2 VM baseline leaves StartupDelayInMSec unset, and the reversible probe plus shell-restart evidence keep that missing state consistent on this build.

allowed_values.label: Observed no-delay profile
allowed_values.meaning: The current app, reversible probe, and shell-restart evidence all align on 0 as the no-delay startup profile for the Explorer Serialize path.

app_current_implementation.notes: Guest-side reversible probe on Win25H2Clean confirmed the current app write and restore cycle for StartupDelayInMSec.

windows_defaults.label: Observed Win25H2Clean baseline
windows_defaults.applies_to: Current 25H2 builds where the Explorer Serialize baseline is still unset

states.rationale: Keep StartupDelayInMSec unset, which is the current observed Win25H2Clean baseline.

windows_defaults.notes: This is the current observed shell baseline, not a published Microsoft shell policy contract.

recommended_profiles.label: Observed default

states.rationale: Use the current observed missing baseline on the Explorer Serialize path.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You want the current observed startup baseline on Win25H2Clean.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You want startup apps to launch immediately after sign-in.
tradeoffs: Still stays research-gated because Microsoft does not publish the registry contract directly.

recommended_profiles.label: Current app profile
intended_for: People who want startup apps to launch sooner after sign-in

states.rationale: Use the current no-delay profile that the VM reversible probe and shell trace both back.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You want startup apps to launch without the current staggered delay.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You prefer the current observed Explorer baseline or startup staggering reduces sign-in load on your system.
tradeoffs: Can make startup feel quicker on some systems.
Can also make sign-in heavier by stacking more startup load at once.
recommended_profiles.notes: App-ready under the current v3.1 converged-vm lane rather than an official-policy lane.

evidence.title: Repo system research notes for startup delay
evidence.summary: The repo documents StartupDelayInMSec under HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\Serialize and notes that setting it to 0 removes the startup delay.

evidence.title: Current app implementation
evidence.summary: The provider writes StartupDelayInMSec = 0 for the current tweak.

evidence.title: Our Ghidra headless string search on explorer.exe for Serialize
evidence.summary: A headless Ghidra string search on explorer.exe for `Serialize` produced three matches. This is string-reference support for the Explorer Serialize path on the current build, not a decompiled registry-read function for StartupDelayInMSec.

evidence.title: Win25H2Clean reversible probe - Explorer startup delay
evidence.summary: Guest reversible probe toggled StartupDelayInMSec to 0 and restored the missing baseline under Explorer\Serialize, confirming the current app write and restore cycle.

evidence.title: VM Procmon trace - Explorer shell restart reads StartupDelayInMSec
evidence.summary: The record keeps the Procmon layer as the repo-friendly placeholder for the original Win25H2Clean Explorer shell-restart capture while StartupDelayInMSec was toggled to 0 and restored. That preserves runtime corroboration for the live startup-delay surface on this build without checking in a text export of the raw `.pml`.

evidence.title: Win25H2Clean WPR trace - Explorer shell restart with StartupDelayInMSec missing and 0
evidence.summary: A bounded WPR lane captured Explorer restarts for both the missing baseline and the app's value 0, and the shell recovered cleanly in both states.

decision.why: The current v3.1 record stays Class A on a converged-vm basis: the reversible probe confirms missing -> 0 -> missing, the record still carries Procmon shell-restart corroboration for the same path, the Ghidra search confirms the Explorer Serialize string path, and the bounded WPR lane exercised both missing and 0 with clean shell recovery. Microsoft still does not publish the raw registry contract, so this is build-specific runtime evidence rather than official-policy evidence.

open_questions: Can a primary Microsoft source for StartupDelayInMSec be captured?
Should the Procmon shell-restart lane be re-exported into a checked-in text artifact later?

validation_proof.exact_quote_or_path: vm-batch-probe-20260320.json: system.disable-startup-delay before=__MISSING__, after_apply=0, after_restore=__MISSING__; startup-delay-0.summary.json: before missing, applied 0, restored missing, shell_after explorer/sihost/ShellHost/ctfmon true
validation_proof.notes: Retroactive v3.1 pass relies on the checked-in reversible probe, the startup-delay WPR summaries for both missing and 0, the explorer.exe Ghidra string-search output, and the repo-friendly Procmon placeholder note for the original shell-restart capture. Microsoft still does not publish StartupDelayInMSec as a primary registry contract.
